item.title: call
item.description:
To call in poker is to match the current wager so the player can remain in the hand [@britannica2026poker]. Calling neither ends the action nor increases the price for later players. It accepts the current price of continuation.

Calling matters because poker decisions are comparative. A player calls when the expected value of continuing at the current price is better than folding, but not strong enough to justify a raise. This relation between hand strength, draw potential, and price is one of poker’s central analytical problems [@sklansky1999].

item.title: fold
item.description:
To fold in poker is to abandon the current hand and give up any claim to the pot [@britannica2026poker]. Folding ends the player’s participation in that hand immediately.

Folding is one of poker’s core strategic actions because it limits losses. A player does not need to continue just because chips are already committed. Strong poker depends as much on disciplined folding in weak positions as on aggressive betting in strong ones [@sklansky1999].

item.title: raise
item.description:
To raise in poker is to increase the current wager [@britannica2026poker]. A raise keeps the player in the hand while forcing later players to pay more if they want to continue.

Raising is the action that gives poker much of its strategic depth. It can build the pot when the raiser expects to be ahead, pressure weaker ranges into folding, or represent a stronger hand than the player actually holds. Because a raise changes both information and price, it does more than move chips [@sklansky1999].

item.title: showdown
item.description:
A showdown in poker is the stage at the end of a hand where the remaining players reveal their cards to determine who wins the pot [@britannica2026poker]. A hand reaches showdown only if betting does not end it earlier.

Showdown matters because it is only one of two ways a poker hand can end. The other is collective folding by all but one player. This is why poker cannot be reduced to hand-ranking alone: the threat of future showdown influences betting long before cards are actually revealed.

item.title: hand
item.description:
A hand in poker is a set of cards held by or assigned to a player, used to determine the outcome of a round [@britannica2026poker]. The term has two related meanings: the specific cards a player holds and a complete round of play from deal to showdown.

Poker hands are ranked by a hierarchy that reflects the probability of being dealt each combination from a standard deck. The rarest combinations rank highest: a royal flush is the strongest hand in most variants, while a high card is the weakest. Between these extremes, the standard ranking runs through pairs, two pair, three of a kind, straights, flushes, full houses, four of a kind, and straight flushes. Some poker variants modify this hierarchy or introduce additional categories.

item.title: pot
item.description:
The pot in poker is the total amount wagered by all players during a hand [@sklansky1999]. It accumulates through antes, blinds, and bets made across the round’s betting streets. The player who wins the hand - either by holding the strongest cards at showdown or by making all other players fold - collects the pot.

Pot size influences strategy at every decision point. A large pot relative to the cost of calling a bet gives a player favorable pot odds, making it mathematically correct to continue with a wider range of hands. A small pot makes aggressive betting riskier because there is less to gain. Skilled players manipulate pot size deliberately: building it when they hold strong hands to maximize value, and keeping it small when their hand is marginal or when they want to see additional cards cheaply.
